original_hypothesis.title: Airdrop Contract Failure in burn() Can Permanently Lock NukeFund ETH
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: NukeFund.nuke() calls nftContract.burn(tokenId). Inside burn(), if airdropContract.airdropStarted() returns false, it calls airdropContract.subUserAmount(). If this external call reverts (buggy airdrop contract, gas limits, or ownership transfer issues), the entire nuke transaction reverts. Fund balance is deducted before burn in nuke(), but since it's atomic, the revert restores it. However, if this condition persists, NO tokens can be nuked, permanently locking all NukeFund ETH.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The burn function creates a hard dependency on the airdrop contract for all nuking operations. Since the airdrop contract code is not in scope but ownership is transferred to nft, any failure in subUserAmount permanently bricks the entire nuke payout mechanism, effectively stealing all ETH in NukeFund.
deployment_assumptions: Airdrop contract's subUserAmount could revert under certain conditions
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Deployment script transfers airdrop ownership to nft: airdrop.transferOwnership(await nft.getAddress()). Airdrop contract code not provided for analysis.

proof_of_signal.pattern: burn() calls airdropContract.subUserAmount without try/catch; revert propagates to caller (nuke)

preconditions: airdropContract.airdropStarted() returns false (airdrop not yet started)
airdropContract.subUserAmount() reverts for any reason (bug, underflow, access control)
NukeFund has accumulated ETH from minting

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No try/catch on airdropContract.subUserAmount in burn(); revert propagates to nuke()
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: If airdrop subUserAmount reverts, all nuke operations fail permanently
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: All ETH in NukeFund becomes permanently inaccessible to all users

economic_questions: Can the airdrop contract's subUserAmount revert under any conditions?
Is there a path to start the airdrop that would bypass the subUserAmount call?
What is the expected NukeFund balance at risk?
validation_requirements: Test burn with various airdrop states
Verify whether starting the airdrop resolves the blocking condition

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A - denial of service, not active theft
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Permanent until airdrop is started or contract upgraded

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All NFT holders expecting to nuke for ETH

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Airdrop contract interface and implementation (not in scope)

validation_result.mechanism: Missing try/catch on external airdrop call in burn() creates hard dependency blocking nuke()
validation_result.impact_summary: If airdropContract.subUserAmount() reverts for any reason before the airdrop starts, all nuke operations are permanently blocked, locking ETH in NukeFund.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The NukeFund.nuke() function calls nftContract.burn(tokenId) which in turn calls airdropContract.subUserAmount() when the airdrop hasn't started yet. This external call is made without try/catch error handling, meaning any revert in the airdrop contract's subUserAmount function propagates up through burn() and causes the entire nuke() transaction to revert. Since the airdrop contract code is not in scope, we cannot verify whether subUserAmount could revert, but the calling code has a clear defect: it creates an unprotected hard dependency on an external contract for a critical protocol function (nuking/claiming ETH). Common causes of revert could include arithmetic underflow (if tracked amounts don't match), access control issues, or any bugs in the out-of-scope airdrop contract. The defect exists in the calling code regardless of the called code's behavior.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: TraitForgeNft.sol burn() function at lines ~134-141 makes an unprotected external call to airdropContract.subUserAmount(). NukeFund.sol nuke() function calls nftContract.burn(tokenId) which triggers this external call chain. There is no try/catch, no error handling, and no fallback mechanism. The entire nuke operation is atomically dependent on the airdrop contract's subUserAmount succeeding.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The call to airdropContract.subUserAmount() has no try/catch. If this external call reverts for any reason (bug, underflow, access control, gas), the entire burn transaction reverts. Since NukeFund.nuke() calls this burn function, any failure here blocks all nuke operations, effectively locking ETH in the NukeFund.

nuke() calls nftContract.burn(tokenId) which internally calls the airdrop contract. If the airdrop call reverts, the entire nuke() transaction reverts atomically (fund -= claimAmount is also reverted). While atomicity prevents fund accounting issues, it means NO tokens can be nuked if the airdrop contract malfunctions.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls NukeFund.nuke(tokenId) - passes validation checks
3. nftContract.burn(tokenId) is called - goes to TraitForgeNft.burn()
7. If subUserAmount REVERTS -> entire call chain reverts including nuke()
8. fund state change is rolled back, ETH remains locked in NukeFund
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: NukeFund.nuke() -> TraitForgeNft.burn() -> airdropContract.subUserAmount(). The subUserAmount call at step 6 has no try/catch wrapper. Any revert in the airdrop contract propagates all the way back to nuke(), causing a complete transaction failure. Since this affects ALL nuke attempts (not just a specific token), it effectively locks all ETH in the NukeFund until either: (a) the airdrop is started via startAirdrop() bypassing the subUserAmount call, or (b) the airdrop contract issue is resolved. If the airdrop contract is permanently broken, the ETH is permanently locked.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Airdrop contract's subUserAmount could revert under certain conditions
config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment script shows: await airdrop.transferOwnership(await nft.getAddress()). The airdrop contract code is not provided for analysis. The deployment script does not show startAirdrop() being called during deployment, meaning the airdrop starts in 'not started' state where the vulnerable code path is active.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: This is primarily a denial of service scenario rather than an active attack. The condition triggers if: (1) airdropContract.airdropStarted() returns false (the normal state before owner calls startAirdrop), AND (2) airdropContract.subUserAmount() reverts. The airdrop contract is out of scope, but common causes for revert include: arithmetic underflow if subUserAmount tries to subtract more than a user's balance (e.g., if a token was transferred and the original owner no longer has sufficient tracked amount), access control issues despite ownership transfer, or contract bugs. The vulnerability exists in the calling code's failure to handle external call failures gracefully.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH - If triggered, all nuke operations across the entire protocol are blocked. The NukeFund accumulates ETH from all minting (90% of mint fees), trading, and forging activities. With no ability to nuke, this ETH becomes inaccessible to all NFT holders. The mitigation path (starting the airdrop) exists but requires owner intervention and may have its own prerequisites or side effects. The core defect is the missing error handling on an external call in a critical code path.
recommended_mitigations: Wrap the airdropContract.subUserAmount() call in a try/catch block in TraitForgeNft.burn() to prevent airdrop contract failures from blocking burns and nukes
Similarly wrap the airdropContract.addUserAmount() call in _mintInternal() and _mintNewEntity() in try/catch to prevent minting from being blocked
Consider adding a flag or admin function to bypass the airdrop accounting in burn() as an emergency measure
Add the same try/catch protection to airdropContract.airdropStarted() calls, as these could also revert
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy all contracts per deployment script. 2. Deploy a mock Airdrop contract where subUserAmount() always reverts. 3. Set this mock as the airdrop contract on TraitForgeNft. 4. Mint some tokens and send ETH to NukeFund. 5. Attempt to nuke a token - observe it reverts. 6. Verify no tokens can be nuked while the condition persists. 7. Call startAirdrop() and verify nuking works again (bypasses the subUserAmount call).
uncertainties: The actual Airdrop contract implementation is not in scope - we cannot verify specific conditions under which subUserAmount would revert
The owner could potentially call startAirdrop() to bypass the vulnerable code path, but this depends on whether the airdrop is ready to be started and may have unintended consequences
The airdropContract.airdropStarted() call itself could also revert if the airdrop contract is buggy, which would affect even more code paths (minting, forging, receiving ETH in NukeFund)
additional_testing_needed: Review the Airdrop contract implementation (not in scope) to determine specific revert conditions in subUserAmount()
Test burn() behavior with various airdrop contract states (paused, zero balance, access control changes)
Test whether starting the airdrop resolves the blocking condition without side effects
Verify the same vulnerability pattern in _mintInternal() and _mintNewEntity() with addUserAmount()
